message:
feat(home): add tutorial CTA section to homepage

feat(tutorial): refine Step 7 prompt and content

- Adds prominent tutorial discovery CTA to homepage with learning path badges
- Refines Step 7 with cleaner prompt format and improved troubleshooting guidance
- Removes unused motion import for smaller bundle

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

+ Ready to Apply the Method? +

+

+ Three learning paths from quick start to multi-agent orchestration. + Apply Brenner's scientific method to your own research questions. +

+
+ + ~30 min Quick Start + + + Agent-Assisted + + + Multi-Agent Cockpit + +
+
+ +
+ Start Tutorial + +

@@ -3,15 +3,15 @@ /** * Agent-Assisted Step 7: Agent Runs the Brenner Loop * - * Full loop execution: generate tests, rank by potency, identify exclusions. + * Agent produces the full research artifact: discriminative tests + potency ranking, + * updated hypothesis slate, assumption ledger, and adversarial critique. * * @see brenner_bot-w5p6 (Tutorial Path: Agent-Assisted Research) */ import * as React from "react"; import { useRouter } from "next/navigation"; -import { motion } from "framer-motion"; -import { TutorialStep, TutorialCodeBlock, ProTip, Important, Warning } from "@/components/tutorial"; +import { TutorialStep, TutorialCodeBlock, Important, ProTip } from "@/components/tutorial"; import { useTutorial } from "@/lib/tutorial-context"; import type { TutorialStep as TutorialStepType } from "@/lib/tutorial-types"; @@ -26,27 +26,30 @@ const stepData: TutorialStepType = { title: "Agent Runs the Brenner Loop", estimatedTime: "~10 min", whatYouLearn: [ - "How to design discriminative tests that distinguish hypotheses", - "What makes a test 'potent' vs. weak", - "How to rank tests by discriminative power", + "How to force discriminative (not confirmatory) tests", + "How potency checks prevent weak experiments", + "What a complete Brenner research artifact looks like", ], whatYouDo: [ - "Give your agent the loop execution prompt", - "Review the generated discriminative tests", - "Understand the potency rankings", + "Give the full-loop prompt to your agent", + "Inspect the tests for discriminative power", + "Ask for revisions if anything is missing", ], troubleshooting: [ { - problem: "Tests all seem to confirm rather than discriminate", - solution: "Ask: 'What result would make me abandon hypothesis X?' If you can't answer, the test isn't discriminative.", + problem: "Tests are all 'collect more data'", + solution: + "Ask the agent to propose tests where at least two hypotheses predict different outcomes and to include a potency check for each test.", }, { - problem: "Agent generated too many tests to evaluate", - solution: "Ask it to rank by potency and focus on the top 3. Quality over quantity.", + problem: "Agent ignores third alternatives", + solution: + "Explicitly demand at least one genuine third alternative and require it to be treated as a first-class hypothesis with its own predictions.", }, { - problem: "Tests seem infeasible", - solution: "Add constraints: 'Design tests I could run with [available resources]. What's the minimal experiment that discriminates?'", + problem: "Output is unstructured or rambly", + solution: + "Ask for a strict sectioned artifact (headings) and a table for discriminative tests with columns for predictions under each hypothesis.", }, ], }; @@ -64,281 +67,132 @@ export default function AgentAssistedStep7() { tutorial.goToStep(6); }, [tutorial]); - const loopPrompt = `Now run the full Brenner loop on my hypothesis slate. Here are my hypotheses: + const loopPrompt = `I want you to run a full Brenner Loop on my research question using the artifacts you already generated. -[PASTE YOUR HYPOTHESIS SLATE FROM STEP 6] +## Inputs -For each pair of hypotheses that need distinguishing, design discriminative tests: +### Research question +[PASTE YOUR REFINED QUESTION FROM STEP 5] -## 1. Discriminative Test Design +### Hypothesis slate +[PASTE THE HYPOTHESIS SLATE FROM STEP 6] -For each test, specify: -- **What you would do**: The actual observation or experiment -- **Predicted outcomes**: What each hypothesis predicts you'd see -- **Discrimination logic**: Why this test distinguishes between hypotheses -- **Feasibility notes**: What resources/access would be needed +### Assumption ledger +[PASTE THE ASSUMPTION LEDGER FROM STEP 6] -A good discriminative test has different predictions for different hypotheses. If all hypotheses predict the same outcome, the test isn't discriminative. +### Third alternatives +[PASTE THE THIRD ALTERNATIVES FROM STEP 6] -## 2. Potency Ranking +### Scale checks / plausibility notes (if any) +[PASTE ANY SCALE CHECK NOTES FROM STEP 6] -Rank your tests by "potency" - how many hypotheses they can exclude: -- **High potency**: Could eliminate 2+ hypotheses with a single result -- **Medium potency**: Eliminates 1 hypothesis, narrows options -- **Low potency**: Provides supporting evidence but doesn't exclude +--- -For each test, note: "If result is A, exclude H1 and H3. If result is B, exclude H2." +## Requirements (Brenner disciplines) -## 3. Exclusion Test (⊘) Application +1) **Discriminative tests only (⊘ Exclusion Test):** + - Propose tests where at least two hypotheses predict different outcomes. + - Each test must specify: what observation would EXCLUDE which hypothesis. -For the top 3 tests, work through the Exclusion Test operator: -- What would the "excluded" hypothesis predict? -- Is there any way that hypothesis could accommodate the result? -- How confident would we be in exclusion? +2) **Potency check each test (🎭):** + - If the test comes back null/negative, what do we learn? + - If the test is likely to be ambiguous, rewrite it until it isn't. -## 4. Critical Path +3) **Keep the hypothesis space honest (Σ + ⟳):** + - Ensure hypotheses are not level-mixed (Σ Level Split). + - Ensure at least one genuine third alternative is included (⟳ Object Transpose). -Based on potency rankings, suggest the order in which to run tests: -- Start with highest-potency tests -- Note dependencies (test B only makes sense after test A) -- Identify the "critical path" - minimum tests needed to reach a conclusion +4) **Scale sanity (⊙):** + - Where relevant, include order-of-magnitude or numeric plausibility checks. -Be rigorous. Remember: we're not trying to confirm what we believe. We're trying to eliminate what's false.`; +--- + +## Output format (strict) + +Produce a single markdown artifact with these sections: + +1. **Single triangulated kernel** (3–6 bullets): your best current hypothesis + the single most discriminative next test + why. +2. **Hypothesis slate (revised)**: 4+ hypotheses, each with mechanism + 1–2 key assumptions. +3. **Discriminative tests (ranked)**: a table with: + - Test name + - What you do / measure + - Predictions if H1/H2/H3/... true + - Exclusion logic (what outcome rules out what) + - Potency check (🎭) + - Feasibility (time/cost/skill) +4. **Assumption ledger (updated)**: grouped by hypothesis and by type (theoretical / methodological / background). +5. **Adversarial critique**: attack the framing; propose 1–2 alternative framings. +6. **Recommended next steps**: the next 3 actions, ordered by discriminative power. + +Take your time and be ruthless about discriminative power.`; + + const handleBack = () => { + tutorial.goToPrevStep(); + router.push("/tutorial/agent-assisted/6"); + }; + + const handleNext = () => { + tutorial.completeAndAdvance(); + router.push("/tutorial/agent-assisted/8"); + }; return ( router.push("/tutorial/agent-assisted/6")} - onNext={() => { - tutorial.completeAndAdvance(); - router.push("/tutorial/agent-assisted/8"); - }} + onBack={handleBack} + onNext={handleNext} >
- {/* The Core Concept */} - -
- - ⊘ - -

- Discriminative, Not Confirmatory -

-
-

- This is the heart of Brenner's approach. Most researchers design experiments - to confirm their hypothesis. We design experiments to exclude hypotheses. - The goal is to find tests where different hypotheses predict different outcomes. -

- +

+ This is where the method becomes operational: your agent turns the hypothesis slate into + discriminative tests, ranks them by potency, and produces a complete research artifact. +

- {/* What Makes a Test Discriminative */} -
-

What Makes a Test Discriminative?

- -
-
-

- Confirmatory (Weak) -

-

- "If my hypothesis is true, I should see X." -

-

- Problem: Seeing X doesn't rule out other hypotheses that also predict X. -

-
- -
-

- Discriminative (Strong) -

-

- "H1 predicts X, H2 predicts Y. Observe which happens." -

-

- Power: Either result eliminates at least one hypothesis. -

-
-
-
- - {/* The Prompt */} -
-

The Loop Execution Prompt

-

- Paste your hypothesis slate where indicated and give this to your agent: -

- -
+ + Don't accept weak tests. The point is not to "gather more information" — it's to + design tests that can actually exclude hypotheses. + - {/* Potency Explained */}
-

Understanding Potency

+

The Full-Loop Prompt

- Not all tests are equal. Potency measures how much a test can narrow - your hypothesis space: + Copy this prompt, fill in the bracketed sections from Steps 5–6, and give it to your agent:

- -
- {[ - { - level: "High Potency", - color: "oklch(0.72_0.19_145)", - example: "A single observation eliminates multiple hypotheses", - scenario: "If we see X, both H1 and H3 are impossible. If we see Y, H2 is ruled out.", - }, - { - level: "Medium Potency", - color: "oklch(0.75_0.15_85)", - example: "Eliminates one hypothesis definitively", - scenario: "If we see X, H2 is ruled out. Otherwise, all remain possible.", - }, - { - level: "Low Potency", - color: "oklch(0.55_0.15_250)", - example: "Provides evidence but doesn't exclude", - scenario: "X is more likely under H1, but H2 and H3 could also explain it.", - }, - ].map((item, i) => ( -
-
- -

- {item.level} -

-
-

{item.example}

-

"{item.scenario}"

-
- ))} -
+
- - Beware of tests that seem powerful but actually aren't. "If I see X, it - supports my hypothesis" is not discriminative. Ask: "What would I see if - my hypothesis is wrong?" - - - {/* Example Output */}
-

Example: Good Test Design

-
-

## Test T1: Temporal ordering

-

- What: Measure whether X changes before Y, or Y before X, in time-series data. -

-

- Predictions:
- - H1 (X→Y): X changes precede Y changes
- - H3 (Y→X): Y changes precede X changes
- - H4 (Z→both): Both change simultaneously after Z -

-

- Potency: HIGH - distinguishes between 3 hypotheses -

-

- Exclusion logic: If X precedes Y, exclude H3. If Y precedes X, exclude H1. - If simultaneous, investigate Z (supports H4). -

-
-
- - - The critical path is your research roadmap. A well-designed critical path - minimizes wasted effort by running high-potency tests first and only - doing follow-up tests when the first results narrow the field. - - - - If your agent generates a test and you can't immediately see what result - would exclude a hypothesis, the test probably isn't discriminative. Push - back: "What specific result would rule out H2?" - - - {/* Review Checklist */} -
-

Review Checklist

-

- Before proceeding, verify your agent's output: -

+

What to Look For

  • - - Each test has explicit predictions for each hypothesis + • + Tests that make different predictions under different hypotheses.
  • - - At least one test could eliminate 2+ hypotheses + • + A potency check for every test (what you learn if the result is null).
  • - - Potency rankings are justified, not just asserted -
    • -
  • - - Critical path shows clear decision points -
    • -
  • - - Tests are feasible with resources you have access to + • + A revised hypothesis slate that stays mechanistic and includes a real third alternative.
- {/* Success Criteria */} -
-

Success Criteria

-

- You're ready to proceed when you have: -

-
    -
  • - - At least 3 genuinely discriminative tests -
    • -
  • - - Clear potency rankings with reasoning -
    • -
  • - - A critical path showing which tests to run first -
    • -
  • - - Exclusion logic for each high-potency test -
    • -
-
+ + If the agent produces a huge artifact, ask it to additionally output a one-screen + "single triangulated kernel" summary: best current hypothesis + best next test + why. + - {/* Next Step Preview */}

- Next up: In Step 8, you'll - review the complete research artifact and verify it meets quality standards - before using it to guide actual research. + Next up: In Step 8, you'll do a human review + using a checklist that catches the most common failure modes.
